phrasal_verbs.meaning: practise and improve your skills or knowledge of something
change your opinion or decision because someone has persuaded you to agree with them
think of something such as an idea or a plan
accept something and try to deal with it
be able to understand something or solve a problem; understand what someone is like
suddenly have an idea; discover something by chance
see, hear or understand someone or something with difficulty; suggest, imply
think carefully about something over a period of time
learn the truth about something by considering all the separate bits of information
solve a confusing or complicated problem by thinking carefully about it
get information on a particular subject by reading a lot about it
study something very hard, especially for an examination
understand and remember something; accept something as real or true; trick someone
consider a problem or decision carefully
consider the facts about something in an organised and thorough way
invent or imagine something, especially an excuse

conclusion: come to / arrive at / reach a conclusion
perspective: from another / a different / sb's / etc perspective
principle: stand by / stick to your principles
a matter / an issue of principle
question: beg the question
raise the question (of)
a/no question of
in question
out of the question
without question
beyond question
some question over / as to / about
awkward question

idioms.meaning: if success goes to your head, it makes you think that you are better or more important than you really are
be able to think quickly and make sensible decisions
not knowing very much about something, because other people are keeping it secret from you
know the important facts or the real situation

idioms.idiom: not have a leg to stand on
idioms.meaning: not have any way of proving that you are right about something

idioms.idiom: not see the wood for the trees
idioms.meaning: used for saying that someone cannot understand what is important because they are thinking too much about small details
guess what is happening or what something means based on what you have seen or heard
taking a very short/long time to understand or realise something
argue or worry about very small details or differences that are not important
spend some time thinking about the situation you are in before you decide what to do next

questions.sentence: You're being far too **optimistic / pessimistic** about your chances — I'm sure you'll pass the exam.
The documentary was clearly **biased / prejudiced** in favour of the company it was supposed to be examining.
I'm rather **dubious / cynical** about politicians — I never believe a word they say.
It's slightly **naïve / sceptical** to think you can learn a language fluently in just two months.
She came up with an **ingenious / plausible** solution that none of us had ever thought of.
The long list of technical terms completely **baffled / discriminated** the students.
It's difficult to **assess / assume** the full impact of the new policy so soon after its introduction.
Can you really **justify / speculate** spending so much money on a new computer when the old one works fine?
I would **estimate / deduce** that around a hundred people attended the lecture.
Don't **reflect / presume** that everyone shares your point of view — it's not always the case.

B.title: Complete using the correct form of the words
B.instructions: Complete using the correct form of the words in the box. More than one word might fit in each gap, but try to use all the words.

It's almost impossible to …………… when there are so many distractions around you.
She's seriously …………… changing her degree course after the first semester.
How long do you …………… it'll take to write up the research project?
I don't …………… there's any point in submitting the essay after the deadline.
She spent the whole evening …………… whether to accept the scholarship or stay at her current university.
We simply refuse to …………… failure at this stage of the project.

C.title: Correct the wrong words in bold
C.instructions: Each of the words in bold is in the wrong sentence. Write the correct words on the lines.

questions.sentence: The aspiring writer stared at a blank page for hours — she had absolutely no **faith** for her new novel.
I had a strong **inspiration** that something about the offer wasn't quite right.
With no written records to go on, the archaeologists were forced to rely entirely on **hunch**.
People assume Edison was simply a natural **guesswork**, but he said his achievements were ninety-nine percent hard work.
She faced a real **genius**: stay in her stable job or take the risk of starting her own business.
The party's political **dilemma** was built around the principle that all citizens deserve equal rights.
Despite every setback, she had absolute **ideology** in her students' ability to succeed.
It's a fascinating **idea** that the more time you spend on social media, the lonelier you can feel in real life.
From the very first session, the counsellor had a **paradox** that this student needed additional support.
She always trusted her **notion** when judging people's characters, and she was almost never wrong.
Without any solid data, the whole business plan was based on nothing more than the **intuition** that demand would grow.

D.title: Choose the correct word
D.instructions: Choose the correct word (A, B, C or D) to complete each gap in the text.
D.passage:
The debate about whether schools should focus more on academic knowledge or on practical life skills continues to divide educators. Critics of the current system argue that the national (1) ...... places too much emphasis on preparing students for standardised tests and too little on developing their ability to (2) ...... information critically. In a typical school week, students spend hours (3) ...... for exams, only to forget most of what they have learned shortly afterwards.

What gets lost, argue the critics, is the development of genuinely (4) ...... thinkers — students who can engage in deep analysis and independent reasoning. Universities frequently report that first-year students struggle with (5) ...... because they have never been taught how to work without constant guidance. Many are also unaware of what constitutes (6) ...... — they have no idea where the line between borrowing someone else's ideas and simply copying their work actually lies.

Supporters of the current system (7) ...... that it is not the role of schools to prepare students solely for higher education, but to equip them for life as a whole. A broad and balanced curriculum, they argue, gives all students an equal (8) ...... on which to build. However, students who arrive at university (9) ...... of basic study skills will struggle from the start. Perhaps the most (10) ...... solution would be a curriculum that develops both knowledge and independent thinking in equal measure.

questions.sentence: The national (1) ...... places too much emphasis on preparing students for standardised tests.
Developing their ability to (2) ...... information critically.
Students spend hours (3) ...... for exams, only to forget most of what they have learned.
The development of genuinely (4) ...... thinkers — students who can engage in deep analysis.
First-year students struggle with (5) ...... because they have never been taught how to work without constant guidance.
Many are also unaware of what constitutes (6) ......
Supporters of the current system (7) ...... that it is not the role of schools to prepare students solely for higher education.
A broad and balanced curriculum gives all students an equal (8) ...... on which to build.
Students who arrive at university (9) ...... of basic study skills will struggle from the start.
Perhaps the most (10) ...... solution would be a curriculum that develops both knowledge and independent thinking.

E.title: Match to make sentences
E.instructions: Match the sentence beginnings (1–7) with the correct endings (A–G).

questions.sentence: I'll need to brush **E** up on my statistics before the research methods module starts.
questions.answer: E – up on my statistics before the research methods module starts.

questions.sentence: He's gradually come **B** round to the idea that distance learning could work for him.
questions.answer: B – round to the idea that distance learning could work for him.

questions.sentence: It's time you faced **G** up to the fact that the exam is going to require serious preparation.
questions.answer: G – up to the fact that the exam is going to require serious preparation.

questions.sentence: We could barely make **A** out what the lecturer was saying from the back of the hall.
questions.answer: A – out what the lecturer was saying from the back of the hall.

questions.sentence: It took the whole class over an hour to puzzle **D** out the answer to the final problem.
questions.answer: D – out the answer to the final problem.

questions.sentence: She spent the whole weekend reading **C** up on the history of the Renaissance for her essay.
questions.answer: C – up on the history of the Renaissance for her essay.

questions.sentence: Can you come **F** up with a better argument than that?
questions.answer: F – up with a better argument than that?

F.title: Write one word in each gap (phrasal verbs)
F.instructions: Write one word in each gap so that each second sentence has a similar meaning to the first sentence.

questions.sentence:
I need to study more about Italian Renaissance art before my trip.
I need to read …………… on Italian Renaissance art before my trip.

My colleagues eventually persuaded me that online collaboration has real advantages.
My colleagues eventually brought me …………… to the idea that online collaboration has real advantages.

Nobody could understand why the professor cancelled the seminar at such short notice.
Nobody could …………… out why the professor cancelled the seminar at such short notice.

You really should improve your spoken French before the oral examination.
You really should brush …………… on your spoken French before the oral examination.

She invented a brilliant plan to help students who were falling behind.
She thought …………… a brilliant plan to help students who were falling behind.

We finally understood how to solve the complex logic puzzle after an hour.
We finally puzzled …………… how to solve the complex logic puzzle after an hour.

I'm going to think carefully about your proposal before I give you an answer.
I'm going to mull …………… your proposal before I give you an answer.

He has to accept the fact that success in this field demands real dedication.
He has to face …………… to the fact that success in this field demands real dedication.

I studied all the key vocabulary the night before the test.
I swotted …………… on all the key vocabulary the night before the test.

G.title: Phrasal verbs with out
G.instructions: Phrasal verbs with out, such as puzzle out, are often connected to the idea of finding information. Which of these phrasal verbs with out are also connected to this idea?

questions.sentence: Did you **work out** how to fix the error in the programme?
The children all **poured out** of the classroom when the bell rang.
I've been **checking out** whether the library stocks the textbook we need.
The reporter managed to **ferret out** the truth behind the financial scandal.
My phone battery **ran out** just before the end of the lecture.
I can't **fathom out** why she changed her mind at the very last minute.
His voice was completely **drowned out** by the noise of the crowd outside.
I'm going to **sound** my tutor **out** about submitting the assignment a day late.
This particular essay really does **stand out** from all the others in the group.
Scientists are still trying to **seek out** new explanations for the phenomenon.

H.title: Write one word in each gap (collocations)
H.instructions: Write one word in each gap.

questions.sentence: I think you may be …………… the misapprehension that this course has no final exam. It does.

The telescope was slightly …………… of focus, so the images weren't as sharp as expected.

I'll always associate the smell of old books …………… our childhood visits to the local library.

His belief …………… the power of education helped him overcome very significant obstacles.

Learning a foreign language really does put your own mother tongue …………… perspective.
From my point …………… view, the current grading system is fundamentally unfair to students.
The volunteers were recruited …………… the basis that they had relevant previous experience.
…………… balance, I think the new approach to teaching has been a success.
The impact of social media on students' wellbeing has not been taken …………… account.
…………… conclusion, I would like to thank everyone who contributed to the research project.

I.title: One word for three sentences
I.instructions: For each question, write one word which can be used in all three sentences.

questions.sentence:
She couldn't keep her …………… during the long lecture and kept drifting off.
The marketing team decided to shift their …………… to a younger audience.
The photo is slightly out of …………… — could you take it again?

Would you …………… if I opened the window? It's rather warm in here.
She couldn't make up her …………… whether to study medicine or law.

From my point of ……………, the experiment was an unqualified success.
He refused to change his …………… no matter what evidence was presented to him.
They climbed to the hilltop to get a better …………… of the whole valley.

I couldn't make …………… of the instructions at all — they were completely unclear.
It makes perfect …………… to revise regularly rather than cramming the night before.
She has a wonderful …………… of humour, even in the most stressful situations.

The research team came to the …………… that further study was absolutely necessary.
Don't jump to …………… — we still don't know the full story.

J.title: Write one word in each gap (text)
J.instructions: Write one word in each gap.
J.passage: Last year I started a distance learning course in psychology. I made up my (1) …………… to try it after a colleague recommended it highly. I took into (2) …………… the fact that I had very little free time, but decided to give it a go. However, I was under the (3) …………… that it would be less demanding than traditional study. In (4) ……………, I would have to say it was at least as challenging. Looking on the bright (5) ……………, I was able to study entirely at my own pace. On (6) ……………, I would recommend distance learning to anyone who is sufficiently motivated to stick with it.

K.title: Key word transformation
K.instructions: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence, using the word given. Do not change the word given. You must use between three and eight words, including the word given.

questions.sentence:
The idea of learning a language in just three months had never crossed her mind.
**occurred** The idea of learning a language in just three months had …………………………………………… her.

I finally decided that distance learning was not the right approach for me.

The teacher explained in detail what the students had been doing during the week.

The advertisement made people believe the product was far better than it actually was.
**false** The advertisement …………………………………………… the product was far better than it actually was.

**strike** In this job you need to …………………………………………… analytical thinking and creativity.

He spoke very quietly because he did not want to upset the other students.
**consideration** He spoke very quietly …………………………………………… the other students.

Marcus has been devoted to learning classical guitar for many years.
**spent** Marcus …………………………………………… classical guitar.

The career counsellor said the entrance examinations would be highly competitive.
**warned** The career counsellor …………………………………………… the entrance examinations would be highly competitive.

L.title: Complete idioms

The noise from the construction site next door is driving me completely round the ……………

M.title: Word formation
M.instructions: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals.

questions.sentence: There was considerable …………… when the lecturer handed out the wrong reading lists to the class. **CONFUSE**
The politician spoke with such …………… that even her opponents found it hard to disagree. **CONVINCE**
She stood in complete …………… as she listened to news of his extraordinary discovery. **BELIEVE**
The …………… of the modern university system as we know it dates back to medieval Europe. **CONCEIVE**
The underlying …………… behind the experiment was that regular sleep significantly improves memory. **ASSUME**
His …………… as a mathematician was already apparent by the time he was a teenager. **BRILLIANT**
It is completely …………… why the school decided to cut funding for the arts programme. **EXPLAIN**
The defendant was found not guilty by reason of …………… and was sent to a secure hospital. **SANE**
Her …………… knew no limits — she could always find a creative solution to any problem. **IMAGINE**
It seems completely …………… to spend three hours commuting when you could work from home. **LOGIC**

N.title: Negative prefixes with il-
N.instructions: The prefix il-, as in illogical, is often used to make a positive word negative. Which of the words in bold in the following sentences are negative forms of positive words? Write YES or NO.

questions.sentence: The note had become completely **illegible** after years of exposure to damp.
The architect showed us the **illustrations** of the new university building.
Driving through a red traffic light is not just **illegal** but also extremely dangerous.
She had an **illustrious** reputation as one of the finest academic minds of her generation.
The new law was widely criticised as deeply **illiberal** and an attack on personal freedoms.
Several witnesses testified about the **illegality** of the company's accounting practices.
The statue cast a long shadow that created an eerie **illusion** of movement in the moonlight.
Her speech was truly **illuminating** and left the audience with a great deal to think about.
The couple's marriage was later declared **illegitimate** due to an administrative error in the registration.

O.title: Word formation (Learning text)
O.instructions: Complete the text by writing the correct form of each word in capitals.
O.passage:
The …(1)… (CONCEIVE) of critical thinking as a teachable skill has gained widespread acceptance in recent years. The …(2)… (ASSUME) that intelligence is fixed at birth has been largely discredited by modern neuroscience. It is …(3)… (DOUBT) true that learning new things actively changes the physical structure of the brain.

However, many students struggle with the …(4)… (CONFUSE) surrounding different study techniques. Which methods are truly effective, and which produce only the …(5)… (BELIEVE) impression of genuine learning? These are important questions. Students who feel …(6)… (CONVINCE) by a teacher's explanation often leave thinking they have understood, only to discover later that they cannot apply what they have learned.

A …(7)… (DECIDE) learner, by contrast, will commit to a method and persevere long enough to see real results. Such a student is not put off by problems that at first seem …(8)… (EXPLAIN). With time, patience, and a truly …(9)… (IMAGINE) approach to problem-solving, most challenges can be overcome. Ultimately, the most …(10)… (DEFINE) measure of learning is not a test score, but the ability to think and act independently.

questions.sentence: The …(1)… (CONCEIVE) of critical thinking as a teachable skill has gained widespread acceptance.
The …(2)… (ASSUME) that intelligence is fixed at birth has been largely discredited.
It is …(3)… (DOUBT) true that learning new things actively changes the structure of the brain.
Many students struggle with the …(4)… (CONFUSE) surrounding different study techniques.
Which methods produce only the …(5)… (BELIEVE) impression of genuine learning?
Students who feel …(6)… (CONVINCE) by a teacher's explanation often leave thinking they have understood.
A …(7)… (DECIDE) learner will commit to a method and persevere long enough to see results.
Such a student is not put off by problems that at first seem …(8)… (EXPLAIN).
With time, patience, and a truly …(9)… (IMAGINE) approach to problem-solving, most challenges can be overcome.
The most …(10)… (DEFINE) measure of learning is not a test score, but the ability to think and act independently.
